topic.what: MAPS II — the updated European Society of Gastrointestinal Endoscopy (ESGE) guideline for the management of epithelial precancerous conditions and lesions in the stomach, providing structured surveillance recommendations based on OLGA/OLGIM staging.
topic.how: Systematic evidence review and expert consensus to define risk strata, biopsy protocols (Sydney protocol), endoscopic mapping requirements, and surveillance intervals for patients with atrophic gastritis, intestinal metaplasia, and dysplasia.
topic.why_matters: Provides the most widely adopted European framework for risk-stratified gastric cancer surveillance, directly influencing clinical practice for millions of patients with precancerous gastric conditions.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Clinical deployment and adoption of the MAPS II surveillance framework across European gastroenterology practice
characteristics.vs_baseline: Updates and replaces MAPS I (2012) with refined risk stratification and clearer surveillance intervals
characteristics.evidence: ESGE consensus guideline based on systematic review of available evidence
characteristics.context: European clinical practice; other regions may follow different guidelines (e.g., AGA in the US)

good_for: Risk-stratifying patients with gastric precancerous conditions
Standardizing endoscopic surveillance protocols for atrophic gastritis and intestinal metaplasia
Guiding biopsy mapping protocols using Sydney system
not_for: Non-European settings where alternative guidelines may be more contextually appropriate
Patients without histologically confirmed precancerous conditions
Hereditary gastric cancer syndromes requiring distinct management
requires: Standardized biopsy protocol (Sydney system: antrum, incisura, corpus)

novelty: Introduces refined risk strata based on OLGA/OLGIM staging for surveillance interval determination
Explicit recommendation for chromoendoscopy-guided biopsies over random biopsies alone
open_problems: Validation of OLGA/OLGIM as prognostic tools in prospective cohorts across diverse populations
Integration of molecular biomarkers into the risk stratification framework
Cost-effectiveness of chromoendoscopy-based surveillance versus standard white-light endoscopy
